questions.stem: The profit-maximising rule for a firm in any market structure is to produce the output where which condition holds?
options: Average revenue is at its maximum
Marginal revenue equals marginal cost (MR = MC)
questions.explanation: A firm maximises profit by producing where marginal revenue equals marginal cost. Below that output an extra unit adds more to revenue than to cost; beyond it, the extra unit costs more than it earns.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a defining characteristic of perfect competition?
options: There are strong barriers to entry
There are many buyers and sellers, each too small to affect the market price
A single seller controls the market
questions.explanation: Perfect competition features a large number of small buyers and sellers, a homogeneous product, free entry and exit, and perfect information. Each firm is a price taker, too small to influence the market price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A firm in perfect competition is described as a 'price taker', which means it does which of the following?
options: Faces an upward sloping demand curve
Must accept the market price as given
Can change the market price by varying output
Sets its own price above the market
questions.explanation: Because each perfectly competitive firm is tiny relative to the market and sells an identical product, it cannot influence the price. It takes the market price as given and can sell any quantity at that price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The demand curve facing an individual firm in perfect competition is which of the following?
options: Horizontal (perfectly elastic) at the market price
questions.explanation: A price taking firm can sell as much as it likes at the going price but nothing above it, so the demand curve it faces is horizontal at the market price. For this firm, price equals marginal revenue equals average revenue.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: For a perfectly competitive firm, marginal revenue is equal to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Since a price taker sells every unit at the same market price, each extra unit adds exactly that price to revenue. So marginal revenue equals price (and also equals average revenue) in perfect competition.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the long run, firms in perfect competition tend to earn which of the following?
questions.explanation: Free entry and exit drive perfectly competitive firms to normal profit in the long run. If firms earn supernormal profit, new entrants compete it away; if they make losses, some leave until only normal profit remains.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A monopoly is best described as a market with which of the following?
options: A single seller of a product with no close substitutes
Free entry and exit of firms
questions.explanation: A pure monopoly is a market supplied by a single firm producing a product with no close substitutes, protected by high barriers to entry. The firm is the industry.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Compared with a perfectly competitive firm, a monopolist faces a demand curve that is which of the following?
options: Identical to its marginal cost curve
questions.explanation: Because the monopolist is the only seller, the demand curve it faces is the whole downward sloping market demand curve. To sell more it must lower the price, so it is a price maker, not a price taker.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: For a monopolist, marginal revenue is which of the following relative to price?
options: Less than price (the marginal revenue curve lies below the demand curve)
questions.explanation: To sell an extra unit a monopolist must lower the price on all units sold, so the gain in revenue is less than the price of that unit. Hence marginal revenue lies below the demand (average revenue) curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a common source of barriers to entry that can create or protect a monopoly?
options: A very large number of small sellers
Control of an essential resource, patents, or large economies of scale
questions.explanation: Barriers to entry such as legal patents, ownership of a key input, large economies of scale, or government licences keep potential rivals out and allow a monopoly to persist and earn long-run supernormal profit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Relative to a perfectly competitive industry, a monopoly typically does which of the following?
options: Produces the same output at the same price
Produces more output at a lower price
Produces less output and charges a higher price
questions.explanation: Because marginal revenue lies below price, a profit-maximising monopolist restricts output below the competitive level and charges a higher price. This is the basis of the standard criticism of monopoly on efficiency grounds.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Monopolistic competition is a market structure characterised by which of the following?
options: Many firms selling differentiated products with relatively free entry
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition has many firms, each selling a slightly differentiated product (through branding, quality, or location), with low barriers to entry. Each firm has a little market power over its own variety.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Product differentiation in monopolistic competition gives each firm which of the following?
options: A downward sloping demand curve for its own product
questions.explanation: Because its product is distinct from rivals' products, a monopolistically competitive firm faces a downward sloping (though fairly elastic) demand curve. It can raise price a little without losing all its customers.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the long run, firms in monopolistic competition tend to earn which of the following?
options: Exactly the same profit as a monopoly
Only normal profit, because entry competes profits away
questions.explanation: As in perfect competition, low barriers to entry mean new firms enter when supernormal profits exist, shifting each firm's demand left until only normal profit remains. The difference is that firms keep some price-setting power.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An oligopoly is a market structure dominated by which of the following?
options: A very large number of tiny firms
A small number of large, interdependent firms
questions.explanation: Oligopoly is a market supplied by a few large firms. Each is big enough that its decisions affect rivals, so firms are interdependent and must consider how competitors will react to their actions.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The key feature that distinguishes oligopoly from other market structures is which of the following?
options: Mutual interdependence between the few firms
There are no barriers to entry
questions.explanation: The hallmark of oligopoly is interdependence: because there are only a few firms, each must anticipate and react to the others' pricing and output decisions. This strategic interaction is often analysed using game theory.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When oligopolists secretly agree to fix prices or restrict output, this behaviour is known as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Collusion occurs when firms cooperate, for example by fixing prices or sharing the market, to act like a monopoly and raise joint profits. A formal collusive group is a cartel. Such agreements are illegal in many countries.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following lists market structures in order from most competitive to least competitive?
options: Monopolistic competition, perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly
Monopoly, oligopoly, perfect competition, monopolistic competition
Perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, monopoly
Oligopoly, monopoly, perfect competition, monopolistic competition
questions.explanation: From most to least competitive the usual ordering is perfect competition, then monopolistic competition, then oligopoly, then monopoly. Competitiveness falls as the number of firms shrinks and barriers to entry rise.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A firm should shut down production in the short run if the price falls below which of the following?
questions.explanation: In the short run a firm should keep producing as long as price covers average variable cost, because it then contributes something towards fixed costs. If price falls below average variable cost, it loses less by shutting down.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Normal profit is best described as which of the following?
options: The maximum profit a monopoly can earn
The minimum return needed to keep the entrepreneur in the business, counted as a cost
questions.explanation: Normal profit is the minimum reward the entrepreneur needs to stay in the industry rather than move resources elsewhere. It is treated as a cost of production. Profit above this is supernormal (economic) profit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a profit-maximising firm finds that its marginal revenue exceeds its marginal cost at the current output, it should do which of the following?
questions.explanation: When marginal revenue is above marginal cost, producing one more unit adds more to revenue than to cost, raising profit. The firm should expand output until marginal revenue equals marginal cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best real-world example of a market close to perfect competition?
options: A producer of patented branded medicine
A market for a basic agricultural product with very many small farmers
questions.explanation: Markets for basic farm produce, with very many small undifferentiated sellers and easy entry, come closest to perfect competition. The other examples involve a single or dominant supplier with substantial market power.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Non-price competition, such as heavy advertising and branding, is especially associated with which market structures?
questions.explanation: Where products are differentiated and firms have some market power, as in monopolistic competition and oligopoly, firms compete heavily on branding, quality, and advertising rather than on price alone. Perfectly competitive firms sell identical goods, so advertising by one would not pay.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A firm is earning supernormal profit when which of the following is true at its profit-maximising output?
options: Price (average revenue) is above average total cost
Price (average revenue) is below average total cost
Total revenue equals total variable cost
questions.explanation: Supernormal (economic) profit arises when price, which is average revenue, exceeds average total cost at the chosen output. The firm then earns more than the normal profit already included in costs.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Why does a monopolist not have a conventional supply curve in the way a perfectly competitive firm does?
options: Because it never makes a profit
Because its marginal cost is always zero
Because it always supplies an unlimited amount
Because it sets output where marginal revenue equals marginal cost and then reads price off demand, so there is no unique price-quantity relationship
questions.explanation: A competitive firm's supply curve is its marginal cost curve because it equates price with marginal cost. A monopolist instead sets marginal revenue equal to marginal cost and charges what demand will bear, so output depends on the shape of demand, not on a fixed supply curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In perfect competition, productive and allocative efficiency in the long run are associated with which condition?
options: Output is restricted to raise price
Price is set well above marginal cost
Firms produce at minimum average cost and price equals marginal cost
questions.explanation: In long-run perfect competition each firm produces at the lowest point of its average cost curve (productive efficiency) and price equals marginal cost (allocative efficiency). This is the benchmark against which other structures are judged.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
